titleInfo.title: Dhāraṇī and Spells in Medieval Sinitic Buddhism

mods.abstract: He discusses 1. Jingying Huiyuan (523-592) who analysed dhāraṇī in his Mahayana Compendium Dasheng yizhang 大乘義章 (T#1851, 26 fasc.) Following Dharmaksema’s (known as "Great Spell Master"大咒师) translation of the Bodhisattva-bhumi (Pusa dichi jing 菩萨地持经), Huiyuan classified dhāraṇī in four groups: dharma (fa tuoluoni 法陀罗尼), meaning (yi tuoluoni 義陀罗尼), spell technique (zhoushu tuoluoni 咒术陀罗尼) and acquiescence (ren tuoluoni 忍陀罗尼). This taxonom was later also employed by Bodhiruci (fl. 508-527) and Xuanzang (ca. 600-664). Dharma and meaning dhāraṇī are associated with hearing, completely maintaining, and not forgetting the Buddhadharma, the Buddhist teaching. These two types of dhāraṇī may best be thought of as “codes.” Spell-technique dhāraṇī, he explains, rely on spiritual efficacy (shenyan ) and are something bodhisattvas produce to dispel all adversity. Acquiescence dhāraṇī, the final type, allow a bodhisattva to maintain his place on the bodhisattva path. ... Thus, to Huiyuan, the acquisition of dhāraṇī, including magic spells and incantations, is a natural outgrowth of a bodhisattva’s religious cultivation, particularly samadhi or meditative absorption, and is a mark, ornament, or adornment of a bodhisattva’s spiritual attainment. The ability to work wonders is presented as a sign of a true bodhisattva. Prior to his death in 592, Jingying Huiyuan commanded his disciples that twice a day, before the morning and afternoon lecture assemblies, the entire congregation of monks and others participating in the lecture should chant the Prajñaparamita or Perfection of Wisdom spell (bore poluomi zhou ) fifty times (T 2060, 50.492c18.). This probably refers to the spell found at the end of Kumarajiva’s translation of the Heart Sutra. 2. Daoshi (ca. 596-683), produced a Buddhist “encyclopedia” called A Grove of Pearls in the Garden of the Dharma (Fayuan zhulin). Two fascicles treat dhāraṇī.

titleInfo.title: Journal of the International Association of Buddhist Studies
